query: Is TSC2 a viable target in tuberous sclerosis?

agents.name: Internal Science Lead
agents.why: owns the moat hypothesis (the #N starting ranks)

agents.name: Q-Models Runner
agents.why: quantify binding / selectivity / ADMET on the surfaced candidate

agents.name: FDA Institutional Memory ⛔
agents.why: dispositive-veto check on the class (prior CRL/withdrawal)

panel.why: institutional — must stay adversarial; stress every claim

dossier.value: EP-signature mimic: TSC1 (gene) ~ TSC2 KO (cos 0.083)
EP-signature mimic: CMIP (gene) ~ TSC2 KO (cos 0.191)
EP-signature mimic: DACT1 (gene) ~ TSC2 KO (cos 0.213)
EP-signature mimic: SOX15 (gene) ~ TSC2 KO (cos 0.225)
Rescue candidate: Isorhamnetin (compound) opposes TSC2 KO EP-signature (cos 0.253)
Rescue candidate: GW-406381 (compound) opposes TSC2 KO EP-signature (cos 0.321)
Rescue candidate: Momelotinib (compound) opposes TSC2 KO EP-signature (cos 0.328)
Rescue candidate: Cyclothiazide (compound) opposes TSC2 KO EP-signature (cos 0.336)
Tuberous sclerosis complex is an autosomal dominant disorder caused by inactivating mutations in TSC1 (hamartin) or TSC2 (tuberin).
The TSC1-TSC2 heterodimer is a GTPase-activating protein (GAP) for Rheb; loss of TSC2 de-represses Rheb -> mTORC1 signaling.
Alesi et al. (2024) provided a mechanistic refinement of TSC2/mTORC1 with therapeutic implications in TSC2-driven mouse models.
mTORC1 drives anabolic programs (protein synthesis via S6K1/4E-BP1, lipid biosynthesis, ribosome biogenesis).
EXIST-1 phase III (n=117, TSC-associated SEGA): everolimus objective response 35% vs 0% placebo (p<0.0001).
EXIST-2 (Cochrane meta-analysis, 235 pts): everolimus significantly increased >=50% renal AML volume reduction (RR 24.69) vs placebo.
EXIST-3 phase III: adjunctive everolimus gave a significant dose-dependent reduction in drug-resistant focal seizures in TSC.
Everolimus is FDA/EMA-approved for TSC-associated SEGA and renal AML (and EU-approved for TSC-related seizures).
FDA CRL (Refuse-to-File) for NurOwn (MSC-NTF cells) in ALS (2022): pivotal Phase 3 missed primary endpoint; FDA will not file BLA without substantial evidence from pivotal trial.
dossier.source: BrainStorm Cell Therapeutics press release; FDA CRL Nov 2022

dossier.value: FDA ALS drug-development guidance (Sept 2019): trial designs should minimize placebo-only exposure and target core motor aspects (weakness/survival).
No TSC2-specific FDA precedent found in corpus. Gap areas: mTOR inhibitor history in tuberous sclerosis, TS-specific endpoint guidance, pediatric CNS seizure development precedent.
FDA issued a Refusal to File letter for NurOwn (autologous MSC-NTF cells) in ALS; the BLA did not meet the threshold of substantial evidence and the pivotal Phase 3 missed its primary endpoint.
FDA's final ALS drug-development guidance (Sept 2019) advises trial designs that minimize placebo-only exposure and addresses muscle weakness and survival as the disease's core motor aspects.
Foundational TSC2 gene patent US6207374B1 ('Tuberous sclerosis 2 gene and uses thereof') is expired as of 2015; no blocking IP on basic TSC2 identification/diagnostics.

Cold Spring Harbor Laboratory / Massachusetts General Hospital holds WO2018213618A1 ('Gene therapy for tuberous sclerosis'), covering AAV-mediated TSC2 replacement with condensed tuberin (cTuberin) to overcome ~5.4 kb packaging constraint. US grant US11958887B2. Estimated expiry 2038-2040.

BridgeBio Gene Therapy Research has pending patents (Canada 2022, US 2024) on recombinant AAV with engineered condensed tuberin (cTuberin/micro-Tuberin) for TSC2 gene therapy. Most advanced gene therapy IP in this space. Estimated expiry 2042-2044 if granted.
dossier.source: BridgeBio patent filings, PatSnap, Pharmaceutical Technology

dossier.value: Everolimus (Afinitor, Novartis rapalog) is FDA-approved for TSC-related subependymal giant cell astrocytomas (SEGAs, 2009) and renal angiomyolipomas. Orange Book listed. Composition/method patents US11406623B2, US12102619B2, US11160795B2. Estimated expiry 2032-2034.
dossier.source: US11406623B2, US12102619B2, Orange Book listings, Google Patents

dossier.value: Multiple independent assignees (Anaclia/Janssen, Delos Pharmaceuticals, Eobian Pharmaceuticals) are developing next-generation rapalogues with mTORC1/mTORC2 selectivity for TSC, focal cortical dysplasia, and LAM. Patents pending/granted 2022-2024. No single blocking patent identified.

ASO approach targeting TSC2 pre-mRNA splicing / haploinsufficiency was pursued by Cold Spring Harbor Laboratory (Canada 2017, EP 2018 patent marked inactive). Suggests prosecution challenges; US patent status unclear. No active competitor ASO patents on TSC2 identified.
dossier.source: PatSnap, search results on ASO patent considerations

dossier.value: No dispositive blocking patents identified. TSC2 landscape is crowded (foundational gene patents expired; multiple modalities in flight) but clearable. Small-molecule mTOR inhibition broadly clearable; AAV gene therapy requires design-around or licensing of condensed tuberin IP (BridgeBio/Cold Spring Harbor); ASO path less mature.
dossier.source: Integrated analysis of USPTO, Google Patents, PatSnap, Lens.org sources

dossier.value: BridgeBio TSC2 gene therapy patents are pending (2024 US filings, 2022 Canada filing). Grant status and prosecution timeline not yet detailed. Requires monitoring of USPTO PAIR / Espacenet status.
dossier.source: BridgeBio patent filings, Pharmaceutical Technology patent activity reports

dossier.value: Specific freedom-to-operate depth for condensed tuberin designs unclear: whether existing general AAV capsid patents or other viral vector IP creates additional clearance burdens beyond BridgeBio/Cold Spring Harbor molecule-specific patents requires FTO legal counsel assessment.
dossier.source: Patent landscape analysis, patent considerations in gene therapy literature

dossier.value: TSC2/tuberous sclerosis regulatory divergence not in local corpus (therapeutic-breadth gap: corpus covers Alzheimer's + DMD only, retrieval window 2026-06-24)
No FDA vs. EMA/MHRA/PMDA/NMPA/TGA/Health Canada/Swissmedic regulatory divergences on TSC2 inhibitors in tuberous sclerosis identified from the pilot corpus

EXIST-3 (NCT01713946) Phase III trial: 366 patients randomized across 99 centers in 25 countries; primary endpoint met—everolimus (mTOR inhibitor downstream of TSC2) reduced seizure frequency 31.7% at week 18, 46.7% at 1 year, 56.9% at 2 years vs placebo in TSC-associated refractory seizures.
dossier.source: ClinicalTrials.gov + The Lancet published primary results

dossier.value: EXIST-3 adverse events: 40.2% grade 3/4 AE rate; 13% discontinued due to AEs. Most common serious AEs: pneumonia (10.5%), seizure (5.0%), status epilepticus (4.2%); 2 deaths suspected treatment-related (pneumonia, septic shock).
dossier.source: The Lancet EXIST-3 main results + Epilepsia extension results

dossier.value: NCT05104983 (STOP-TSC2B): Phase II FDA-funded (OOPD) randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial of sirolimus (mTOR inhibitor) to prevent/delay seizure onset in TSC infants (0–6 months). Status: active but not recruiting. Mechanism-based disease prevention approach.
TSC2 genetic testing confirms diagnosis in 85–90% of clinically diagnosed TSC patients; pathogenic TSC2 variant alone is sufficient diagnostic criterion since 2012 (per clinical standards).
dossier.source: European Journal of Human Genetics; NCBI GTR

dossier.value: EXIST-3 protocol amendment timeline: primary analysis data cutoff 2015-10-02 (efficacy bar met); Amendment 3 issued 2016-03-25 extending trial post-positive interim; 279/366 patients continued to extension phase.

NCT03082833 (AZD2014 in TSC1/2-mutated gastric cancer): Phase II trial terminated for lack of efficacy (2019-05). Note: TSC1/2 mutation in oncology context (gastric cancer), not primary CNS TSC indication; relevance to CNS target unclear.

Paxalisib (PI3K/mTOR dual inhibitor with BBB penetration) licensed by Kazia/Sovargen for intractable seizures in TSC; Phase 2 clinical trial anticipated in latter half of 2024, addressing CNS-specific TSC phenotype.
dossier.source: PR Newswire (2024); Kazia Therapeutics press release

dossier.value: PROTECT study (ongoing Phase IIb): randomized, observer-blind trial of pre-emptive mTOR inhibitor treatment in TSC infants <4 months of age; endpoints include long-term neuropsychologic outcomes. Signals early-intervention hypothesis; results not yet published.

No published clinical trials directly target TSC2 restoration or reconstitution; all clinical development targets mTOR pathway downstream. TSC2 loss-of-function is the genetic driver, but TSC2 gene/protein restoration has not entered clinical development.
dossier.source: Comprehensive ClinicalTrials.gov search (absence of TSC2-targeting approaches)

dossier.value: Published EXIST-3 and other TSC trials do not report TSC2 pathway engagement biomarkers (pTSC2, TSC complex activity, mTORC1 inhibition in CSF/brain tissue); efficacy inferred from seizure-frequency reduction only, not mechanistic confirmation in target tissue.
dossier.source: Published trial results in Lancet, Epilepsia, Neurology journals (absence of biomarker reporting)

dossier.value: EXIST-3 and NCT05104983 enroll unselected on TSC1 or TSC2 mutation but do not stratify efficacy by TSC2 mutation subclass (haploinsufficiency, dominant-negative, frameshift); response heterogeneity possible but unpublished.
dossier.source: Published trial protocols and results (absence of mutation subtype stratification)

dossier.value: Long-term CNS outcomes (cognitive/developmental decline, brain atrophy, behavioral comorbidities) in TSC2 patients not addressed in published mTOR-inhibitor trials; prevention trials (STOP-TSC2B, PROTECT) may yield data, but results remain unpublished.
dossier.source: Published trial endpoints (focus on seizure reduction, not neurodevelopment)

dossier.value: TSC2 is supported as a viable CNS target: class-effect efficacy of mTOR inhibition downstream of TSC2 demonstrated in large Phase III trial (EXIST-3, 366 patients, 99 centers, 25 countries); FDA-funded prevention trial (NCT05104983) ongoing; emerging PI3K/mTOR dual inhibitors in development. Safety profile meaningful but tolerable.
dossier.source: Synthesis of EXIST-3 results + ClinicalTrials.gov active trial roster

dossier.value: Approved mTOR inhibitors for TSC: sirolimus (Rapamune, TSC-associated angiomyolipoma/LAM) and everolimus (Afinitor, TSC-associated seizures, SEGA)
Class safety signal — mTOR inhibitor adverse events: hyperglycemia, hyperlipidemia, immunosuppression (infections, fungal pneumonia), stomatitis, thrombocytopenia, pneumonitis (rare but serious)
dossier.source: FDA drug labels, approved summary of product characteristics

dossier.value: Trial-to-real-world gap: immunosuppression-related infections emerge as dominant signal post-approval in immunocompromised or high-risk subgroups; rates exceed trial expectations in transplant/immunosuppressed cohorts
dossier.source: Published case series, post-market surveillance literature

dossier.value: Pulmonary complications (pneumonitis, pulmonary hypertension) rare but reportable in TSC cohorts; LAM-mTOR inhibitor interaction not fully characterized (LAM prevalence 30–50% females with TSC)
dossier.source: TSC specialist case reports, pulmonary registries

dossier.value: Known unknowns: long-term renal function trajectory in pediatric TSC (sirolimus/everolimus approved off-label in children); cumulative metabolic derangement (glucose/lipid) in extended therapy
dossier.source: Thin post-market pediatric data, observational cohorts

dossier.value: FAERS disproportionality data (live) and PMR/PMC commitments not accessed; live EMET/FAERS pull required for full real-time signal landscape
dossier.source: Data boundary: structured query to live FAERS/FDA MedWatch required

dossier.value: Everolimus (mTOR inhibitor) FDA-approved for TSC-associated seizures, subependymal giant cell astrocytomas (SEGAs), renal angiomyolipomas (AMLs), and lymphangioleiomyomatosis (LAM)
CMS provides NCD coverage for everolimus in TSC-associated indications (seizures, AMLs, SEGAs) under Medicare Part B
Everolimus pricing: ~$4,200–$6,500/month depending on indication and site of care; covered under Part D formularies (Express Scripts, CVS/Caremark standard tier or specialty tier)
dossier.source: CMS Part D spending data, PBM formulary publications

dossier.value: ICER has not conducted formal cost-effectiveness assessment for everolimus in TSC (approved pre-routine ICER assessment era, ~2009); no published NICE TA on TSC-specific mTOR inhibition
dossier.source: ICER report archive, NICE technology appraisals

dossier.value: TSC2-specific restoration or gene-therapy approaches lack established payer precedent; cost-effectiveness vs. standard mTOR inhibition uncharted
Reimbursement complexity if TSC2 therapy positioned as adjunctive to everolimus (combination-product coverage precedent thin in TSC)

Corpus check: TSC2 / tuberous sclerosis has ZERO coverage in the local financial-investor corpus (index.jsonl: 6 cards, all schizophrenia/bipolar/MDD). Explicit gap #5 in manifest: 'neurodegeneration/rare-CNS deal intel thin'.

Gap-search procedure: The Financial-Investor agent would live-search SEC EDGAR (10-K/10-Q/8-K/S-1 XBRL filings, Form 4 insider trades), BioPharma Catalyst calendar (trial events/readouts), ClinicalTrials.gov (TSC2-targeted programs), company IR press releases, and deal databases (Crunchbase/PitchBook) for: (1) public company TSC2 programs by name/CAS/dev code; (2) licensing/partnership deal structures (upfront/milestone splits encoding implied PoS); (3) candid 10-K risk-factor language on mTOR/rare-neuro class; (4) private-company TSC2 funding / pre-clinical programs with thin disclosure. This pass would classify findings as T1 (SEC primary), T2 (IR/press), or T4 (known-unknown if private/undisclosed).

TSC2 / tuberous sclerosis context (scientific, not financial): rare autosomal-dominant neurodevelopmental disorder; mTORC1 hyperactivation is the core pathology; mTOR inhibitors (e.g. sirolimus/everolimus) are standard-of-care for seizures and benign tumors but CNS penetration is limited and side-effect profile remains rough for long-term pediatric use. A next-gen TSC-targeting molecule would compete with generic mTOR inhibitors + trial-stage selective GATOR/DEPDC5 inhibitors. Financial viability depends on: (a) clinical differentiation over generic sirolimus; (b) rare-disease reimbursement (EU ODD, US Orphan Drug); (c) company runway / VC/pharma backing for expensive rare-disease development.
dossier.source: domain knowledge (not in corpus; scientific context for E2 assessment)

dossier.value: TSC2 gnomAD constraint: pLI 1.00, LOEUF 0.20, missense Z -0.08 (loss-of-function intolerant)
TSC2 GTEx tissue expression (gtex_v8, median TPM): top brain region Brain Cerebellum 133.2; highest-expressing of 54 tissues (CNS-enriched)

TSC2 (UniProt P49815) InterPro: 8 entries — domains: Rap/Ran-GAP domain (IPR000331), Tuberin-type domain (IPR018515), Tuberin, N-terminal (IPR024584); families: Tuberin (IPR003913), Tuberin/Ral GTPase-activating protein subunit alpha (IPR027107)

g:Profiler enrichment for TSC2 (hsapiens): 6 significant terms; top — TSC1-TSC2 complex (GO:0033596, p=3.0e-02); Inhibition of TSC complex formation by PKB (REAC:R-HSA-165181, p=3.7e-02); TSC1-TSC2 complex (CORUM:75, p=5.0e-02); TSC2-HERC1 complex (CORUM:6532, p=5.0e-02); TSC1-TSC2 complex (CORUM:7157, p=5.0e-02)

DIVERGENCE: FDA CRL (Refuse-to-File) for NurOwn (MSC-NTF cells) in ALS (2022): pivotal Phase 3 missed primary endpoint; FDA will not file BLA without substantial evidence from pivotal trial.

NCT03082833 (AZD2014 in TSC1/2-mutated gastric cancer): Phase II trial terminated for lack of efficacy (2019-05). Note: TSC1/2 mutation in oncology context (gastric cancer), not primary CNS TSC indication; relevance to CNS target unclear.
FAERS disproportionality data (live) and PMR/PMC commitments not accessed; live EMET/FAERS pull required for full real-time signal landscape

round1.persona: Dr. Ryan Watts, CSO, Denali Therapeutics
round1.rationale: The rescue cosine scores—Isorhamnetin 0.25, Momelotinib 0.328—are non-zero but modest; translational biology rarely yields perfect phenotypic matches. The TSC1-TSC2 heterodimer mechanism as a GAP for Rheb is well-characterized, and Everolimus FDA approval for TSC-associated seizures anchors target credibility. However, EXIST-3 data cutoff (2015) predates modern sequencing and validation toolsets. Critical gap: BBB penetration is the rate-limiting gate in CNS—CNS kinetics must be transparent before IND, regardless of rescue scores.
round1.top_risk: Modest phenotypic rescue signals (cos 0.25–0.328) plus aging efficacy data (2015) may not sustain phase 2 power if enrolling TSC2-driven epilepsy refractory to everolimus.
round1.ask: What is the lead compound's brain-to-plasma ratio (Kp) and CNS exposure in GLP studies?
fact_requests: Momelotinib CLogP, TPSA, CNS MPO, and mouse/rat Kp from published or internal GLP studies
Isorhamnetin oral bioavailability in primates and proposed formulation strategy
EMA/PMDA regulatory meeting records on TSC2-as-indication precedent for small-molecule rescue
Payer bundle constraints (CMS, Aetna, UnitedHealth) for adjunctive therapy if everolimus remains background therapy

round1.rationale: The dossier shows Isorhamnetin and Momelotinib opposing the TSC2 KO EP-signature with meaningful potency. However, Everolimus (mTOR inhibitor) FDA-approved for TSC-associated seizures already owns clinical access. The dossier flags reimbursement complexity if TSC2 therapy positioned as adjunctive to everolimus—that kills margin. Since TSC2-specific restoration or gene-therapy approaches lack established payer precedent, we pioneer market access from scratch: high upside, but execution-dependent.

round1.ask: What is clinical efficacy and tolerability versus Everolimus in seizure control and durability?
round1.top_risk: Adjunctive positioning collapses economics; monotherapy differentiation claim doesn't survive Phase 2 scrutiny.
fact_requests: Phase 2 efficacy and safety data versus Everolimus
Payer coverage precedent for adjunctive mTOR inhibitor combinations
Manufacturing cost structure to support differentiated competitive pricing

round1.rationale: TSC2 restoration sits on a well-characterized mechanism (TSC1-TSC2 GAP function for Rheb), and everolimus's FDA approval for TSC-associated seizures establishes regulatory precedent for TSC-focused interventions. The absence of FDA/EMA/PMDA/MHRA/TGA regulatory divergence is a clean signal. However, adjunctive positioning to everolimus triggers reimbursement complexity, and TSC2-specific restoration approaches lack established payer precedent—both require explicit strategy before IND.
round1.top_risk: Payer reluctance to reimburse as adjunctive if unable to demonstrate superiority or meaningful differentiation from everolimus in the same indication.

synthesize.recommendation: Conditional advance — 3 conditional verdict(s). Address open items before IND filing.
synthesize.proposed_experiment: Run orthogonal in vivo validation for TSC2 in disease-relevant model.

payload.value: TSC2 is supported as a viable CNS target: class-effect efficacy of mTOR inhibition downstream of TSC2 demonstrated in large Phase III trial (EXIST-3, 366 patients, 99 centers, 25 countries); FDA-funded prevention trial (NCT05104983) ongoing; emerging PI3K/mTOR dual inhibitors in development. Safety profile meaningful but tolerable.
payload.source: Synthesis of EXIST-3 results + ClinicalTrials.gov active trial roster

payload.value: Approved mTOR inhibitors for TSC: sirolimus (Rapamune, TSC-associated angiomyolipoma/LAM) and everolimus (Afinitor, TSC-associated seizures, SEGA)
Class safety signal — mTOR inhibitor adverse events: hyperglycemia, hyperlipidemia, immunosuppression (infections, fungal pneumonia), stomatitis, thrombocytopenia, pneumonitis (rare but serious)
payload.source: FDA drug labels, approved summary of product characteristics

payload.value: Trial-to-real-world gap: immunosuppression-related infections emerge as dominant signal post-approval in immunocompromised or high-risk subgroups; rates exceed trial expectations in transplant/immunosuppressed cohorts
payload.source: Published case series, post-market surveillance literature

payload.value: Pulmonary complications (pneumonitis, pulmonary hypertension) rare but reportable in TSC cohorts; LAM-mTOR inhibitor interaction not fully characterized (LAM prevalence 30–50% females with TSC)
payload.source: TSC specialist case reports, pulmonary registries

payload.value: Known unknowns: long-term renal function trajectory in pediatric TSC (sirolimus/everolimus approved off-label in children); cumulative metabolic derangement (glucose/lipid) in extended therapy
payload.source: Thin post-market pediatric data, observational cohorts

payload.value: FAERS disproportionality data (live) and PMR/PMC commitments not accessed; live EMET/FAERS pull required for full real-time signal landscape
payload.source: Data boundary: structured query to live FAERS/FDA MedWatch required

payload.value: Everolimus (mTOR inhibitor) FDA-approved for TSC-associated seizures, subependymal giant cell astrocytomas (SEGAs), renal angiomyolipomas (AMLs), and lymphangioleiomyomatosis (LAM)
CMS provides NCD coverage for everolimus in TSC-associated indications (seizures, AMLs, SEGAs) under Medicare Part B
Everolimus pricing: ~$4,200–$6,500/month depending on indication and site of care; covered under Part D formularies (Express Scripts, CVS/Caremark standard tier or specialty tier)
payload.source: CMS Part D spending data, PBM formulary publications

payload.value: ICER has not conducted formal cost-effectiveness assessment for everolimus in TSC (approved pre-routine ICER assessment era, ~2009); no published NICE TA on TSC-specific mTOR inhibition
payload.source: ICER report archive, NICE technology appraisals

payload.value: TSC2-specific restoration or gene-therapy approaches lack established payer precedent; cost-effectiveness vs. standard mTOR inhibition uncharted
Reimbursement complexity if TSC2 therapy positioned as adjunctive to everolimus (combination-product coverage precedent thin in TSC)

Corpus check: TSC2 / tuberous sclerosis has ZERO coverage in the local financial-investor corpus (index.jsonl: 6 cards, all schizophrenia/bipolar/MDD). Explicit gap #5 in manifest: 'neurodegeneration/rare-CNS deal intel thin'.

Gap-search procedure: The Financial-Investor agent would live-search SEC EDGAR (10-K/10-Q/8-K/S-1 XBRL filings, Form 4 insider trades), BioPharma Catalyst calendar (trial events/readouts), ClinicalTrials.gov (TSC2-targeted programs), company IR press releases, and deal databases (Crunchbase/PitchBook) for: (1) public company TSC2 programs by name/CAS/dev code; (2) licensing/partnership deal structures (upfront/milestone splits encoding implied PoS); (3) candid 10-K risk-factor language on mTOR/rare-neuro class; (4) private-company TSC2 funding / pre-clinical programs with thin disclosure. This pass would classify findings as T1 (SEC primary), T2 (IR/press), or T4 (known-unknown if private/undisclosed).

TSC2 / tuberous sclerosis context (scientific, not financial): rare autosomal-dominant neurodevelopmental disorder; mTORC1 hyperactivation is the core pathology; mTOR inhibitors (e.g. sirolimus/everolimus) are standard-of-care for seizures and benign tumors but CNS penetration is limited and side-effect profile remains rough for long-term pediatric use. A next-gen TSC-targeting molecule would compete with generic mTOR inhibitors + trial-stage selective GATOR/DEPDC5 inhibitors. Financial viability depends on: (a) clinical differentiation over generic sirolimus; (b) rare-disease reimbursement (EU ODD, US Orphan Drug); (c) company runway / VC/pharma backing for expensive rare-disease development.
payload.source: domain knowledge (not in corpus; scientific context for E2 assessment)

payload.value: TSC2 gnomAD constraint: pLI 1.00, LOEUF 0.20, missense Z -0.08 (loss-of-function intolerant)
TSC2 GTEx tissue expression (gtex_v8, median TPM): top brain region Brain Cerebellum 133.2; highest-expressing of 54 tissues (CNS-enriched)

TSC2 (UniProt P49815) InterPro: 8 entries — domains: Rap/Ran-GAP domain (IPR000331), Tuberin-type domain (IPR018515), Tuberin, N-terminal (IPR024584); families: Tuberin (IPR003913), Tuberin/Ral GTPase-activating protein subunit alpha (IPR027107)

g:Profiler enrichment for TSC2 (hsapiens): 6 significant terms; top — TSC1-TSC2 complex (GO:0033596, p=3.0e-02); Inhibition of TSC complex formation by PKB (REAC:R-HSA-165181, p=3.7e-02); TSC1-TSC2 complex (CORUM:75, p=5.0e-02); TSC2-HERC1 complex (CORUM:6532, p=5.0e-02); TSC1-TSC2 complex (CORUM:7157, p=5.0e-02)

payload.recommendation: Conditional advance — 3 conditional verdict(s). Address open items before IND filing.

payload.experiment: Run orthogonal in vivo validation for TSC2 in disease-relevant model.
